title: A Simple Analytical Solution for Organic Contaminant Diffusion Through a Geomembrane to Unsaturated Soil Liner: Considering the Sorption Effect and Robin-Type Boundary

research.question: Can contaminant diffusion through a thin geomembrane into an unsaturated soil liner be represented by a simple analytical solution that includes sorption and a Robin-type boundary?
research.system: One-dimensional diffusion from constant-concentration landfill leachate through a geomembrane into a semi-infinite unsaturated natural soil liner with depth-dependent water content and linear sorption.
research.inputData: Geomembrane and soil diffusion properties, partition coefficient, layer thicknesses, water-content parameters, retardation factor, depth, elapsed time, and concentration observations for five VOCs from a bench-scale composite-liner experiment.
research.method: Replace transport through the thin geomembrane with a Fick-law Robin boundary, impose a Gardner hydrostatic water-content profile and constant retardation factor, derive the concentration solution, compare benchmarks, calculate sensitivities, and fit experiment data.
research.benchmark: The analytical concentration is compared with an explicit finite-difference flow-and-transport model and the Chen et al. (2019) solution, then evaluated against concentration observations from Park et al. (2012).
research.uncertainty: Finite-difference concentration differences test the hydrostatic and constant-retardation assumptions, SEE and ME quantify experiment prediction errors, and interpretation remains conditional on the one-dimensional equilibrium-profile model and selected replicate data.
research.conclusion: The Robin-boundary analytical solution is simpler to evaluate than the compared existing solution, represents sorption through retardation, agrees with the reported comparisons, and fits the selected bench-scale VOC observations for transport-parameter estimation.

narrative.throughline: Follow dissolved contaminant from landfill leachate across a thin geomembrane boundary into an unsaturated sorbing soil profile, where a compact analytical concentration field can be checked and fitted.
narrative.audienceStartingPoint: A geomembrane slows contaminant movement, while diffusion and sorption govern how dissolved compounds enter an underlying soil liner.
narrative.stakes: Ignoring unsaturated water-content variation or sorption can misrepresent how far leachate contaminants migrate toward groundwater.
narrative.resolution: A Robin boundary and retardation-aware analytical field connect the physical liner to numerical agreement, parameter sensitivity, and the selected composite-liner observations.

background.premise: Leachate may diffuse through the GM and enter the underlying natural soil liner.
background.visibleEvidence: A landfill leachate reservoir sits above a thin geomembrane with contaminant particles crossing into the underlying liner.
background.whyNow: The animation needs a concrete contaminant pathway before introducing a boundary model.
background.boundary: The paper addresses diffusion through an intact geomembrane rather than advective leakage through defects.
background.sourceExcerpt: The leachate however may diffuse through the GM and enter the underlying natural soil liner.
background.visualObject: Landfill leachate above a geomembrane and soil liner
background.motionPurpose: Trace contaminant particles from the leachate to the geomembrane surface.
background.stepDetail: Reveal the leachate reservoir, the thin geomembrane, the soil liner, and a downward diffusion path.
background.transitionBridge: The path enters a soil state that differs from the saturated liners used in earlier studies.

background.premise: The target contaminant pathway continues through the GM to the unsaturated soil liner.
background.visibleEvidence: Contaminant particles cross the GM and enter a soil block identified only as unsaturated.
background.whyNow: This identifies the receiving medium for the contaminant pathway.
background.boundary: The abstract establishes only migration into an unsaturated soil liner.
background.sourceExcerpt: migration through the GM to the unsaturated soil liner
background.visualObject: Thin geomembrane above an unsaturated soil liner
background.motionPurpose: Trace the contaminant pathway through the GM and stop at the receiving liner.
background.stepDetail: Reveal the GM, move contaminant particles through it, and stop them at the unsaturated-liner entry.
background.transitionBridge: The established pathway now exposes the evaluation burden of the prior analytical solution.

background.premise: The existing unsaturated-liner analytical solution consists of two infinite sums.
background.visibleEvidence: Two long series extend across the frame while the desired concentration profile remains hidden.
background.whyNow: The two-series form creates one source-stated part of the evaluation burden.
background.boundary: The premise concerns solution form rather than prediction accuracy.
background.sourceExcerpt: Their solution consists of two infinite sums
background.motionPurpose: Extend both summations until they obscure the desired concentration field.
background.stepDetail: Show two growing summations while preserving the soil profile behind them.
background.transitionBridge: The functions inside those sums introduce a separate root-finding burden.

background.premise: Functions in the existing solution's sums must be evaluated with a root-finding procedure.
background.visibleEvidence: A nonlinear function crosses zero at root markers that must be located before the series can be evaluated.
background.whyNow: Separating root finding from the infinite sums preserves the paper's two-part explanation of evaluation complexity.
background.boundary: The premise describes the evaluation procedure for Chen et al. (2019), not the new solution.
background.sourceExcerpt: in the sums should be evaluated with a root-finding procedure
background.visualObject: Nonlinear function with zero-crossing root markers
background.motionPurpose: Locate roots on the nonlinear function before allowing the series terms to appear.
background.stepDetail: Draw the nonlinear function, mark its zero crossings, and connect each root to a series term.
background.transitionBridge: The thin geomembrane can instead be compressed into an interface relation.

methods.input: One-dimensional geomembrane and unsaturated soil liner domains
methods.output: Coupled concentration equations for the geomembrane and soil liner
methods.validity: The model treats diffusion normal to the liner with separate geomembrane and unsaturated-soil properties.
methods.sourceExcerpt: The 1-D transport equations in the GM and soil liner can be
methods.motionPurpose: Assign a concentration field and vertical flux direction to each material domain.
methods.stepDetail: Place the geomembrane equation on the thin upper strip and the unsaturated-soil equation on the deeper profile.
methods.transitionBridge: The thin upper domain is next reduced to its flux relation.

methods.input: Fick-law geomembrane flux with interface partition and flux continuity conditions
methods.output: Robin-type condition at the top of the unsaturated soil liner
methods.validity: The geomembrane is thin relative to the soil liner so its concentration gradient is approximated by the concentration difference across its thickness.
methods.locator: PDF page 3, Section 2.1 Derivation of Robin-Type condition for GM, Equations 6-8
methods.sourceExcerpt: which is a Robin-type condition or the third-type condition in
methods.visualObject: Thin geomembrane collapsed into a flux-concentration interface
methods.motionPurpose: Compress the membrane thickness into a boundary that retains its diffusive resistance.
methods.stepDetail: Replace the membrane gradient with the top-to-bottom concentration difference and pin the resulting relation at z equals zero.
methods.transitionBridge: The interface now needs the depth-dependent water state below it.

methods.input: Gardner water-content relation and unsaturated soil hydraulic head
methods.output: Exponential water-content profile through the soil liner
methods.validity: The pressure head is under hydrostatic equilibrium and the residual water content is treated as negligible in the analytical profile.
methods.locator: PDF page 3, Section 2.2 Transport model and analytical solution, Equations 9a-9b; PDF page 7, Section 4 Concluding remarks
methods.sourceExcerpt: the pressure head is under hydrostatic equilibrium
methods.motionPurpose: Turn hydrostatic head into a visible vertical moisture profile.
methods.stepDetail: Set the hydraulic-head marker to zero and reshape uniform pore-water shading into the Gardner exponential profile.
methods.transitionBridge: Sorption is attached to contaminant storage within this moisture profile.

methods.input: Linear sorption relation within the unsaturated water-content profile
methods.output: Retardation-aware soil transport equation and Robin condition
methods.validity: The paper assumes constant Ru because the reported water-content change has a minor effect on predicted concentration.
methods.sourceExcerpt: In this study, the Ru is assumed as a constant and
methods.visualObject: Contaminant particles delayed on soil solids
methods.motionPurpose: Slow the mobile contaminant markers while preserving the same soil profile.
methods.stepDetail: Attach a sorbed fraction to soil grains and encode the delayed mobile fraction in the transport equation.
methods.transitionBridge: The boundary and transport equation now define the analytical concentration field.

methods.input: Retardation-aware transport equation with clean initial state remote no-flux condition and Robin boundary
methods.validity: The expression solves Eq. (10) with conditions (2) (4) and (11) under the adopted hydrostatic profile.
methods.sourceExcerpt: Eq. (10) with conditions (2), (4), and (11), has a
methods.visualObject: Closed concentration profile built from complementary error functions
methods.motionPurpose: Reveal a direct depth-concentration field in place of the obscuring infinite sums.
methods.stepDetail: Map the initial and boundary objects into separate complementary-error-function terms and settle them into one concentration profile.
methods.transitionBridge: The analytical field is checked against a transient numerical representation.

methods.input: Unsaturated hydraulic conductivity relation with initial and boundary conditions
methods.output: Transient hydraulic-head model for the soil liner
methods.validity: The numerical check uses the Gardner relative permeability relation with the stated top flux and bottom head conditions.
methods.locator: PDF page 4, Section 3.1 Comparison with finite-difference solution, Equations 15-22
methods.sourceExcerpt: The flow equation for describing the hydraulic head h in the soils is
methods.motionPurpose: Expose transient water-state cells that the hydrostatic analytical profile suppresses.
methods.stepDetail: Distribute hydraulic-head nodes down the soil column and apply the reported top and bottom conditions.
methods.transitionBridge: The computed water state supplies coefficients to the contaminant grid.

methods.input: Soil transport equation with nodewise transient water content
methods.validity: The discretization follows Eq. (1b) by replacing the water-content-dependent retardation expression under the reported stability criteria.
methods.sourceExcerpt: Similarly, Eq. (1b) after replacing Ruwith 1 + / can be dis-
methods.visualObject: Finite-difference concentration cells beneath the Robin boundary
methods.motionPurpose: Advance contaminant concentration cell by cell through the transient water state.
methods.stepDetail: Attach concentration values to the vertical nodes, apply the interface and remote conditions, and expose the explicit update stencil.
methods.transitionBridge: The numerical concentration field can now be placed over the analytical profile.

methods.input: Analytical and finite-difference normalized concentration profiles
methods.output: Good agreement between the two concentration distributions
methods.validity: The comparison uses the paper's reported one-year five-year and ten-year cases with its default parameter set.
methods.sourceExcerpt: distributions predicted by both solutions are in good agreement
methods.visualObject: Overlaid analytical and finite-difference concentration profiles
methods.motionPurpose: Align the two profiles so their reported concentration agreement is directly visible.
methods.stepDetail: Overlay matching-time concentration profiles and retain a separate difference trace below them.
methods.transitionBridge: A second comparison tests whether the Robin boundary reproduces the existing GM treatment.

methods.input: Concentration curves predicted by both solutions including the present Robin-boundary solution and Chen et al. solution
methods.output: Reported concentration-profile agreement across unsaturated-exponent cases
methods.validity: The comparison is reported for the selected exponent range at ten years with a small deviation near the stated depth.
methods.locator: PDF pages 5-6, Section 3.2 Comparison with Chen et al. (2019) solution, Figure 3
methods.sourceExcerpt: The curves predicted by both solutions indicate good agreement
methods.visualObject: Paired concentration profiles from the two analytical solutions
methods.motionPurpose: Overlay the profiles while leaving the small reported near-bottom separation visible.
methods.stepDetail: Draw both solution families on common depth axes and preserve their stated near-bottom deviation.
methods.transitionBridge: With the boundary representation checked the retardation factor can vary independently.

methods.input: Present-solution concentration curve with increasing Ru from one to two
methods.output: Shorter contaminant migration distance for larger Ru
methods.validity: The reported Figure 4 comparison is at ten years and interprets Ru above one as sorption retardation.
methods.locator: PDF pages 5-6, Section 3.3 Effect of the retardation factor on leachate migration, Figure 4
methods.sourceExcerpt: The concentration curve predicted by the present solution decreases with increasing Ru
methods.visualObject: Nested depth-concentration profiles indexed by Ru
methods.motionPurpose: Contract the concentration penetration as the retardation marker increases.
methods.stepDetail: Hold time fixed, vary Ru across the reported range, and show the profile reaching a shorter depth.
methods.transitionBridge: The analysis next asks which of all inputs most strongly changes concentration.

methods.input: Model-dependent concentration output and one perturbed model parameter Pk
methods.validity: The partial derivative is approximated with the paper's finite parameter increment of 10^-3 Pk.
methods.locator: PDF page 5, Section 3.4 Sensitivity analysis, Equations 29a-29b
methods.sourceExcerpt: coefficient is defined as the partial derivative of the model-dependent
methods.visualObject: Parameter perturbation linked to a normalized concentration response
methods.motionPurpose: Convert a small parameter change into a signed concentration-response trace.
methods.stepDetail: Shift one parameter by the reported increment and scale the resulting concentration difference into Xk.
methods.transitionBridge: The signed coefficients become relative parameter contributions.

methods.input: Absolute normalized sensitivities for all model parameters
methods.output: Differential importance measure DIMk for each parameter
methods.validity: The paper divides each absolute Xk by the sum of absolute sensitivities across all N model parameters.
methods.locator: PDF page 6, Section 3.4, Equations 30a-30c and Figure 5
methods.sourceExcerpt: where DIMk is the differential importance measure of parameter Pk and
methods.visualObject: Sensitivity magnitudes normalized into parameter shares
methods.motionPurpose: Rescale all sensitivity bars into a common relative-importance total.
methods.stepDetail: Take each absolute sensitivity magnitude and divide it by the visible sum across the eight inputs.
methods.transitionBridge: The model is then taken from parameter influence to measured composite-liner data.

methods.input: Three replicate VOC concentration datasets measured at two depths
methods.output: Five VOC concentration series measured at 0.09 m
methods.validity: The paper excludes the 0.06 m series because variation among its three replicates appears large.
methods.locator: PDF page 7, Section 3.5 Bench-scale composite liner experiment and data analysis
methods.sourceExcerpt: The variation among three replicates for those five species measured
methods.visualObject: Replicate concentration traces at 0.06 m and 0.09 m
methods.motionPurpose: Dim the high-variation shallow traces and retain the selected deeper replicate.
methods.stepDetail: Arrange traces by depth, expose the reported shallow replicate variation, and keep Replicate 2 at 0.09 m.
methods.transitionBridge: The retained traces are recovered from the published experiment figure.

methods.input: Selected Park et al. (2012, Fig. 7) concentration traces
methods.output: Digitized time-concentration observations for five VOCs
methods.validity: The paper states that the selected datasets are read from Park et al. Figure 7 with WebPlotDigitizer.
methods.sourceExcerpt: datasets are read from Park et al. (2012, Fig. 7) using the WebPlotDi-
methods.visualObject: Published plot resolving into digitized concentration points
methods.motionPurpose: Turn the retained printed traces into explicit time-concentration points.
methods.stepDetail: Place sampling markers on each retained VOC trace and transfer them to a clean time axis.
methods.transitionBridge: The analytical concentration field can now be adjusted against those points.

methods.input: Digitized VOC concentration observations and analytical concentration solution
methods.output: Estimated Ru Sg Du Dg and unsaturated-exponent values
methods.validity: The present solution is coupled to the Levenberg-Marquardt algorithm in Mathematica NonlinearModelFit.
methods.visualObject: Analytical VOC curves pulled toward measured concentration points
methods.motionPurpose: Adjust the transport controls until each model curve follows its selected observations.
methods.stepDetail: Expose the five parameter controls and move the corresponding analytical curve toward each VOC point series.
methods.transitionBridge: Residual distances from the fitted curves become prediction-error measures.

methods.input: Residuals between fitted concentrations and selected observations
methods.output: SEE and ME values for each VOC fit
methods.validity: The study uses the standard error of estimate and mean error (ME) defined by Yeh (1987) to assess prediction errors.
methods.sourceExcerpt: mate (SEE) and mean error (ME) given by Yeh (1987) are used to assess
methods.visualObject: Residual stems resolving into SEE and ME labels
methods.motionPurpose: Gather point-to-curve residuals into the two reported error measures.
methods.stepDetail: Draw residual stems for each VOC series and collect them into aligned SEE and ME readouts.
methods.transitionBridge: The fitted curves and errors return to the original composite-liner pathway.

return.visualObject: Original landfill geomembrane and unsaturated soil profile with five fitted VOC concentration traces at 0.09 m
return.claim: For the selected bench-scale datasets the present analytical solution gives good fits to measured concentrations and can estimate transport parameters without the additional batch tests used in the comparison study.
return.claimBoundary: This return is limited to the five reported VOCs from Replicate 2 at 0.09 m and inherits the paper's one-dimensional diffusion hydrostatic-equilibrium constant-retardation semi-infinite-soil and Robin-boundary assumptions.
return.locator: PDF page 7, Section 3.5, Figure 6 and Section 4 Concluding remarks
return.sourceExcerpt: The figure shows good fits for the predictions from the present solution to the measured data, indicating that the present solution can be used to estimate the transport parameters without conducting additional batch tests as did in Park et al. (2012).

visual.primaryObject: Landfill leachate diffusing through a geomembrane into an unsaturated soil liner
visual.accentObject: Robin flux-concentration interface at the geomembrane bottom
visual.measuredQuantity: Depth-dependent and time-dependent VOC concentration in the unsaturated liner
visual.geometryNotes: Keep the landfill GM and unsaturated soil cross section continuous across scenes; collapse the thin GM into a Robin boundary, preserve depth-oriented concentration profiles, show sorption on soil grains, and attach uncertainty or error marks to the numerical difference and experiment residual objects.
